ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for raw pig tail as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for specific pork cuts is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of pig tail is approximately 100 g, representing one segment of tail with bone, cartilage, and fat intact before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Pig tail, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pig tail, raw (cr_id F06090020). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork tail, raw (NDB No. 10193) where HPB does not report these fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pig tail, raw contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06090020). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. glycemic_index set to -1 (sentinel: not applicable / not measurable for this food type). Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI entry exists for pig tail as it is a negligible-carbohydrate animal product.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~12.2 g/100 g) with moderate sodium (63 mg/100 g) in raw form; sodium increases substantially with typical braised preparations using soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Raw pig tail has a low sodium load (63 mg/100 g) and a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (349 mg K vs 63 mg Na), but its high saturated fat content contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure; hypertensive individuals should limit portion size, avoid high-sodium braising sauces, and prefer steaming or boiling over braising with soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content eliminates direct glycaemic impact, but very high total fat (33.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~12.2 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pig tail, raw contains no carbohydrates and will not raise blood glucose directly, making it glycaemically neutral; however, its very high saturated fat content can impair insulin sensitivity over time, so individuals with type 2 diabetes should consume it in small portions infrequently and avoid sweet braising sauces containing rock sugar, hoisin, or maltose that add significant carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high total fat (33.5 g/100 g), saturated fat (~12.2 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and cholesterol (97 mg/100 g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pig tail, raw is a high-fat pork cut with substantial saturated fat, trace trans fat, and significant dietary cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease or elevated LDL cholesterol should treat this as an occasional food only, limit portions to 50 g or less, and prepare it by boiling and discarding the cooking fat rather than braising in fat-retaining sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and arachidonic acid from pork fat promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6); trace trans fat further amplifies systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit pig tail due to its high saturated fat and arachidonic acid content, which can exacerbate joint inflammation and trigger flares, particularly during active disease periods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pig tail, raw refers to the uncooked tail portion of the domestic pig (Sus scrofa domesticus), comprising a combination of skin, subcutaneous fat, cartilage, and bone segments. In its raw state, it displays a pale pink to off-white colour with visible fat layers and cartilaginous rings along the vertebral segments. The tail is a gelatinous, collagen-rich cut that becomes tender and sticky when slow-cooked, braised, or pressure-cooked. It is widely consumed across Singapore, Southeast Asia, the Caribbean, and the Southern United States. In Singapore, pig tail is commonly braised with soy sauce, dark soy sauce, rock sugar, and spices to produce a rich, glossy, umami-forward dish. Nutritionally, raw pig tail is energy-dense at 378 kcal per 100 g, with high total fat (33.5 g), moderate protein (17.75 g), and zero carbohydrate. Its saturated fat content (~12.2 g/100 g) and cholesterol (97 mg/100 g) make it a food of concern for cardiovascular and metabolic health. Sodium in the raw form is low at 63 mg/100 g, but cooking methods involving soy sauce dramatically increase sodium load. The high collagen content contributes to a gelatinous mouthfeel after cooking and is sometimes cited in traditional contexts for joint health, though clinical evidence for this is limited. Pig tail is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, or kosher diets. It is gluten-free and dairy-free in its raw form.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06090020) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (pork tail, raw, NDB 10193), which is an approved secondary source. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated between the two approved sources.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — pig tail, raw contains no carbohydrates as it is a pure meat and cartilage product with no plant-derived components. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal product containing no plant fibre. Saturated fat (~12.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (~15.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (~3.6 g/100 g), and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork tail, raw (NDB 10193) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; these are documented as adapted values. glycemic_index set to -1 (sentinel: not applicable / not measurable); pig tail, raw contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making glycemic index not applicable for this food — confirmed per University of Sydney GI Database consultation which holds no GI entry for negligible-carbohydrate animal products of this type. gi_category set to not_applicable. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin and mineral panel beyond those reported by HPB is not available in the HPB Foodo database for this specific entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy does not have a complete vitamin panel for pork tail specifically. FoodEx2 code A0CKP assigned as the closest defensible match for pig tail, raw within the FoodEx2 pork meat hierarchy. SNOMED CT code not assigned due to absence of a specific concept for raw pig tail as a food product in SNOMED CT. Halal status is false as pork is not permissible under Islamic dietary law.

Value is numeric as required by the scoring model.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03311349, pig tail food product). FoodEx2 code A0CKP assigned for pork tail raw. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. All ontology entries are defensible and not invented.
